FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 11,875 participants ages 9-10 from 21 U.S. sites, NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1519271.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data; series of correlation and regression analyses.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in PLoS ONE 2021; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: the STQ breaks screen time into six types including 'texting on cellphone, tablet, or computer' and 'visiting social media sites.' Supplementary Tables S6-S18 (weekday) and S19-S31 (weekend) run separate regressions of each screen-type subcomponent on every outcome (depression, anxiety, internalizing, externalizing, ODD, conduct disorder, ADHD, academic performance, sleep, peer relationships) — all individual-trait-level outcomes. Texting and social media are phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcomes, yielding qualifying coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized betas, t-statistics, and p-values reported across Tables 2-5 and S6-S31 for screen time predicting individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression analyses report numeric estimates (standardized beta coefficients) throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 requires longitudinal design, which is absent here.
notes: Headline analyses use total screen time composite as IV; per-type breakouts (including texting and social media as separate predictors) are in supplement tables S6-S31. The per-type breakouts satisfy Step 1 of FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study cohort substudy; N=67 ABCD participants (ages 11-12) at year-2 follow-up from 4 geographically dispersed ABCD sites; ABCD Release 3.0 (DOI: 10.15154/1519007).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: descriptive statistics, ANOVA, and correlations on passively sensed smartphone app use data collected over 4 weeks.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JMIR Mental Health 2021; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate found pairing a phone/SM-specific variable with an individual-trait-level outcome. All analyses either assess: (a) correlation between self-report and objective passive sensing (r=0.49) as a measurement-validity check — other side is not an individual-trait outcome but a measurement method; (b) ANOVA of app category use (streaming, communication, gaming, social media, SMS, reading) by demographic categories (sex, age, race/ethnicity, income, geographic location) — other side is demographic only; (c) pre/post self-report comparisons. No mental health, cognitive, behavioral, sleep, academic, or other individual-trait outcomes are analyzed. The other side of every analysis is either measurement validity or demographics. FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

notes: Paper is a feasibility/descriptive study of passive smartphone sensing methodology in an ABCD subsample. No individual-trait outcomes analyzed in relation to phone/SM use.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study release 2.0, N=11,875 youth ages 9-11 from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; negative binomial regression of 24-hour movement guideline combinations on CBCL internalizing/externalizing outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health 2021; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No per-modality phone/SM-specific coefficient found anywhere in the paper. All regressions use an aggregate 'recreational screen time' composite as the exposure (measured in hours/day), categorized as meeting/not meeting the <=2h/day guideline. No separate estimate for texting, social media, video chat, or any phone-specific variable is reported in main tables or supplementary tables. Step 2: The composite recreational screen time is computed from the Youth Screen Time Survey and includes: (1) TV watching, (2) video watching (e.g., YouTube), (3) video game playing (platform unspecified), (4) texting on cellphone/tablet/computer, (5) visiting social media sites, (6) video chat. The composite bundles TV, unspecified-platform video games, and video watching alongside texting/SM/video chat — non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, unspecified-platform video games) are included without separate breakout. FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

notes: Screen time is operationalized as a categorical guideline-meeting variable (yes/no for <=2h/day) from a composite including TV, unspecified video games, videos, texting, social media, and video chat. No per-modality breakout in any regression table.
